our_argument_summary: Our review identifies three independent failures: (1) wrong units (rad/s² vs rad/s), (2) 37% numerical discrepancy even with corrected units, (3) circular derivation from assumed dome parameters with no independent measurement. The IAU 2006 value is cited as the benchmark. The monitoring system omission is noted as evidence the dome's author doesn't treat this as testable.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline. WIN-021 is a static entry in api/master.json with value 4.87e-12 rad/s² and formula 'tau/I'. No Python script computes this from dome parameters. No script fetches observational gyroscope or precession data.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Entirely static assertion. No computation exists in the repository — not even a hardcoded pred==obs comparison in monitor.py. The value is a bare string in the API data with no derivation steps.
data_sources_used: None — no external data fetched for this WIN
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None — not monitored at all, so no tolerance or conditional logic.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our detail_evidence correctly states that monitor.py does not include gyroscopic precession in its automated audit. No code-related claims in our text are inaccurate.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py has no domain for gyroscopic precession. The Cycle 1 review confirmed this: 'monitor.py (the 39-domain audit engine) does not include a gyroscopic precession domain.' No pred==obs comparison exists, not even a hardcoded one. The current tag in wins.json says 'hardcoded' — THIS IS WRONG and must be corrected to 'none'. The dome's wins.html page shows a static result string, but that is not a monitoring domain. The distinction matters: 'hardcoded' means monitor.py actively checks pred==obs with both sides baked in; 'none' means monitor.py doesn't touch this WIN at all.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The dome's claimed value (4.87e-12 rad/s²) does NOT match standard physics (7.73e-12 rad/s). The units are wrong and the value is 37% off. This isn't a relabeling of standard physics — it's a failed attempt at an independent prediction.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Earth's axial precession rate has been measured since Hipparchus (~130 BC). The modern value (50.2881 arcsec/yr, IAU 2006) has been known for decades. The dome model adopted a number in the vicinity of the known value and labeled it a prediction.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome claims derivation via tau/I from disc+aetheric parameters, but no script or document in the repository shows the intermediate mathematical steps. No calculation connects disc_radius, firmament_height, or aetheric parameters to the output 4.87e-12. The formula reference is nominal only.

holes_found.description: code_analysis tag mismatch: wins.json has monitoring='hardcoded' for WIN-021, but our own detail_evidence text says 'monitor.py does not include gyroscopic precession in its 39-domain automated audit engine.' The Cycle 1 curmudgeon review confirmed monitor.py has no precession domain. The correct tag is 'none'. This is the same class of error as the WIN-014 tag mismatch (tagged 'live_fetch' when review said 'none'). Since code_analysis tags feed into Part 4.6 aggregate statistics, a wrong tag corrupts our monitoring-illusion counts.
holes_found.recommendation: Change code_analysis.monitoring from 'hardcoded' to 'none' in wins.json for WIN-021. Verify Part 4.6 counts still compute correctly after the fix.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence mentions 'The dome's own monitoring system (monitor.py) does not include gyroscopic precession in its 39-domain automated audit engine' — this is a strong point but the phrase '39-domain' is a hardcoded count that may drift as the dome site evolves. If the dome adds or removes domains, this number becomes stale.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider softening to 'multi-domain automated audit engine' or verifying the current domain count. However, this is a minor issue since the raw-text is a snapshot of the current version.

holes_found.description: The claim field says 'Gyroscopic precession rate' and the finding says 'Units error: dome gives rad/s² (acceleration) not rad/s (velocity).' The finding is good but could be even punchier by including the 37% discrepancy: readers scanning the summary table should see both failures without clicking through.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider expanding the finding to: 'Units error (rad/s² vs rad/s) and 37% value mismatch. No derivation connects dome geometry to the claimed number.'

stronger_arguments.description: The detail_evidence now covers all three major failures (units, value, circularity) which is a significant improvement from Cycle 1. One additional angle: the dome's formula reference 'tau/I' is dimensionally correct for angular acceleration — torque (N·m) divided by moment of inertia (kg·m²) gives rad/s². This means the dome may have correctly computed an angular ACCELERATION but then labeled it as a precession RATE. The physical distinction matters: Earth's precession rate (angular velocity) is approximately constant over human timescales, while the dome's quantity (angular acceleration) would imply the precession rate is changing. These predict observably different things — VLBI measurements confirm the precession rate is near-constant, not accelerating. This could be noted as a fourth failure mode.
sources: IAU 2006 precession model: Capitaine et al. 2003, Astronomy & Astrophysics 412, 567-586

kernel_of_truth.description: Earth genuinely behaves as a gyroscope, precessing due to luni-solar gravitational torques on its equatorial bulge. The tau/I framework is correct classical mechanics for a torque-driven spinning body. The order of magnitude (~10⁻¹² in SI angular units) is in the right neighborhood, and the dome model correctly identifies precession as a fundamental observable worthy of prediction. This is a real, well-measured phenomenon.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Being in the right conceptual neighborhood doesn't save the claim because: (1) the units are wrong for what's being claimed (acceleration vs velocity), (2) the number is 37% off even charitably, (3) no derivation exists connecting dome geometry to this value, and (4) standard physics has measured this quantity to extraordinary precision via VLBI, lunar laser ranging, and spacecraft tracking — methods that presuppose a globe Earth. The dome model cannot simultaneously reject the globe and use precession measurements that depend on globe geometry for their interpretation.

cycle_comparison.changes_since_cycle_1: All three Cycle 1 recommendations were implemented: (1) units error (rad/s² vs rad/s) is now prominently flagged, (2) GP-B comparison replaced with IAU 2006 precession constant, (3) 37% numerical discrepancy is now included. The argument is substantially stronger than Cycle 1.
cycle_comparison.new_issues_found: One major issue: code_analysis.monitoring tag is still 'hardcoded' when it should be 'none'. This was not flagged as a tag correction in Cycle 1 (the review noted the absence from monitor.py but didn't flag the tag mismatch). Two minor issues: '39-domain' hardcoded count and summary finding could be punchier.
cycle_comparison.remaining_from_cycle_1: None of the original holes remain — all were addressed.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 review of WIN-021 finds the detail text substantially improved since Cycle 1. The three major weaknesses identified in Cycle 1 (missing units error, GP-B apples-to-oranges comparison, missing numerical discrepancy) have all been corrected. The current text now presents a strong triple-failure argument: wrong units, wrong value, no derivation.

The one significant remaining issue is a data integrity problem: the code_analysis.monitoring tag in wins.json is set to 'hardcoded' when it should be 'none'. This matters because Part 4.6 of our review computes aggregate statistics from these tags. Every 'hardcoded' tag that should be 'none' inflates our 'monitoring illusion' count (the number of WINs with fake pred==obs checks) and deflates our 'not monitored' count. The Cycle 1 review clearly documented that monitor.py has no precession domain, but the tag correction wasn't flagged as an action item — likely because the Cycle 1 review predated the tag correction pipeline.

The verdict 'Misleading' remains appropriate. While one could argue for 'Refuted by Data' (the value is 37% off), the primary issue is structural: the dome presents a circular derivation with wrong units as a 'confirmed prediction.' The misleading framing — not the factual error per se — is what makes this WIN problematic. The dome didn't get the wrong answer to the right question; it answered the wrong question (acceleration vs velocity) and claimed confirmation. That's a misleading presentation of results, not merely a failed prediction.

A defender might argue: 'The tau/I formula gives angular acceleration, which is what we intended — we're predicting how fast precession accelerates, not the current rate.' This defense fails because: (a) the dome's own description says 'Gyroscopic Precession Rate' not 'acceleration,' (b) VLBI measurements show the precession rate is near-constant over human timescales, so predicting a specific acceleration is predicting something not observed, and (c) even this defense would make the WIN unfalsifiable, since no measurement of precession acceleration at the 10⁻¹² rad/s² level exists to compare against.

citations_verified: IAU 2006 precession value (50.2881 arcsec/yr → 7.726e-12 rad/s) — verified by independent calculation
citations_unchecked: IAU URL (https://www.iau.org/) — generic homepage link, not a direct reference to the 2006 precession constant. The specific reference should be Capitaine et al. 2003 (A&A 412, 567) or the IAU 2006 Resolution B1. The current link works but doesn't directly support the claim.
